title: Angophora costata

infobox.value: or **smooth-barked apple**, is a species of tree that is [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism") to eastern Australia. Reaching 30 m (100 ft) in height, the species has distinctive smooth bark that is pinkish or orange-brown when new and fades to grey with age. Its lance-shaped leaves are arranged in opposite pairs along the stems, with white or creamy white flowers appearing from October to December. The flower buds are usually arranged in groups of three, followed by ribbed, oval or bell-shaped fruit.

***Angophora costata***, commonly known as **Sydney red gum**, **rusty gum** or **smooth-barked apple**, is a species of tree that is [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism") to eastern Australia. Reaching 30 m (100 ft) in height, the species has distinctive smooth bark that is pinkish or orange-brown when new and fades to grey with age. Its lance-shaped leaves are arranged in opposite pairs along the stems, with white or creamy white flowers appearing from October to December. The flower buds are usually arranged in groups of three, followed by ribbed, oval or bell-shaped fruit.

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angophora_costata&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

*Angophora costata* is a tree that typically grows to a height of 30 m (100 ft) and forms a [lignotuber](/wiki/Lignotuber "Lignotuber"). It has smooth pinkish or orange-brown bark that weathers to grey and is shed in small scales. Young plants and [coppice](/wiki/Coppice "Coppice") regrowth have [sessile](/wiki/Sessility_(botany) "Sessility (botany)"), elliptical to egg-shaped leaves [arranged](/wiki/Phyllotaxis#Leaf_arrangement "Phyllotaxis") in opposite pairs with a stem-clasping base, 60–125 mm (2.5–5 in) long and 20–65 mm (0.8–2.6 in) wide. Adult leaves are arranged in opposite pairs, glossy green but paler on the lower surface, lance-shaped or curved, 70–190 mm (2.8–7.5 in) long and 12–35 mm (0.5–1.4 in) wide on a [petiole](/wiki/Petiole_(botany) "Petiole (botany)") 9–25 mm (0.4–1.0 in) long. New leaf growth is strongly tinted with red. The flower buds are arranged on the ends of branchlets on a branched [peduncle](/wiki/Peduncle_(botany) "Peduncle (botany)") 3–25 mm (0.1–1.0 in) long, each branch of the peduncle with usually three buds on [pedicels](/wiki/Pedicel_(botany) "Pedicel (botany)") 3–15 mm (0.1–0.6 in) long. Mature buds are oval to globe-shaped, up to 10 mm (0.4 in) long and 11 mm (0.4 in) wide. There are five [sepals](/wiki/Sepal "Sepal") up to 3 mm (0.1 in) long and the [petals](/wiki/Petal "Petal") are white to creamy white with a green keel, 3–5 mm (0.1–0.2 in) long and 3–6 mm (0.1–0.2 in) wide. Flowering occurs from October to December. The fruit is an oval or bell-shaped [capsule](/wiki/Capsule_(botany) "Capsule (botany)") up to 20 mm (0.8 in) long and wide.[[3]](#cite_note-RBGS-3)[[4]](#cite_note-ABRS-4)[[5]](#cite_note-CANBR-5) New seedlings have petiolate round [cotyledon](/wiki/Cotyledon "Cotyledon") leaves 1.5 cm wide and long.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6)

## Taxonomy and naming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angophora_costata&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Taxonomy and naming")]

Sydney red gum was first formally described in 1788 by German botanist [Joseph Gaertner](/wiki/Joseph_Gaertner "Joseph Gaertner") and given the name *Metrosideros costata* in his book *[De Fructibus et Seminibus Plantarum](/wiki/De_Fructibus_et_Seminibus_Plantarum "De Fructibus et Seminibus Plantarum")*,[[7]](#cite_note-APNI1-7) from material collected by surveyor [David Burton](/wiki/David_Burton_(botanist) "David Burton (botanist)") around Port Jackson.[[8]](#cite_note-Gaertn.-8) The [specific epithet](/wiki/Binomial_nomenclature "Binomial nomenclature") (*costata*) is a Latin word meaning 'ribbed'.[[9]](#cite_note-Sharr-9) English botanist [Richard Anthony Salisbury](/wiki/Richard_Anthony_Salisbury "Richard Anthony Salisbury") named it *Metrisoderos apocynifolia* in his 1796 work *Prodromus stirpium in horto ad Chapel Allerton vigentium*.[[10]](#cite_note-Salisb.-10) Spanish taxonomist [Antonio José Cavanilles](/wiki/Antonio_Jos%C3%A9_Cavanilles "Antonio José Cavanilles") erected the new genus *Angophora* in 1797 in his book *Icones et Descriptiones Plantarum*, giving the species the name *Angophora lanceolata*.[[11]](#cite_note-Cav.-11) English botanist [George Bentham](/wiki/George_Bentham "George Bentham") used this last name in his *[Flora Australiensis](/wiki/Flora_Australiensis "Flora Australiensis")* in 1867.[[12]](#cite_note-Benth.-12)

English botanist [James Britten](/wiki/James_Britten "James Britten") changed the name to *Angophora costata* in the *[Journal of Botany, British and Foreign](/wiki/Journal_of_Botany,_British_and_Foreign "Journal of Botany, British and Foreign")* in 1916.[[13]](#cite_note-APNI-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Britten-14)

In 1986, [Gregory John Leach](/wiki/Gregory_John_Leach "Gregory John Leach") described three subspecies in the journal *[Telopea](/wiki/Telopea_(journal) "Telopea (journal)")* and two names have been accepted by the [Australian Plant Census](/wiki/Australian_Plant_Census "Australian Plant Census"):[[15]](#cite_note-Telopea-15)

* [*Angophora costata* (Gaertn.) Britten  subsp. *costata*](/wiki/Angophora_costata_subsp._costata "Angophora costata subsp. costata")[[16]](#cite_note-APC1-16) has a [glabrous](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/glabrous "wikt:glabrous") [floral cup](/wiki/Hypanthium "Hypanthium") and fruit mostly less than 15 mm (0.6 in) wide;[[3]](#cite_note-RBGS-3)
* [*Angophora costata* subsp. *euryphylla*](/wiki/Angophora_costata_subsp._euryphylla "Angophora costata subsp. euryphylla") [L.A.S.Johnson](/wiki/Lawrence_Alexander_Sidney_Johnson "Lawrence Alexander Sidney Johnson") ex G.J.Leach[[17]](#cite_note-APC2-17) has a hairy floral cup and fruit more than 15 mm (0.6 in) wide.[[3]](#cite_note-RBGS-3) It is found only in a small area including [Putty](/wiki/Putty,_New_South_Wales "Putty, New South Wales"), Howes Valley and Judge Dowling Range in central New South Wales.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) Genetic analysis published in 2021 showed it not to be distinct from subsp. *costata*.[[18]](#cite_note-rutherford21-18)

A third subspecies, subsp. *leiocarpa* was reclassified as a separate species *[Angophora leiocarpa](/wiki/Angophora_leiocarpa "Angophora leiocarpa")*.[[19]](#cite_note-APC3-19) A phylogenetic analysis of the genus published in 2021 confirmed its genetic distinctness from the two *costata* subspecies.[[18]](#cite_note-rutherford21-18)

Recent genetic work has been published showing *[Angophora](/wiki/Angophora "Angophora")* to be more closely related to *Eucalyptus* than *[Corymbia](/wiki/Corymbia "Corymbia")*, and the name *Eucalyptus apocynifolia* has been proposed for this species if it were to be placed in the genus *[Eucalyptus](/wiki/Eucalyptus "Eucalyptus")*.[[20]](#cite_note-20) This has not been taken up by the botanical community.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6)

Common names include Sydney red gum, rusty gum, smooth-barked apple,[[3]](#cite_note-RBGS-3) and smooth-barked angophora.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) Aboriginal people from the Sydney region knew the tree as *kajimbourra*.[[21]](#cite_note-Les-21)

## Distribution and habitat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angophora_costata&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Distribution and habitat")]

*Angophora costata* grows in sandy soil, often over [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") and occurs naturally in Queensland and New South Wales. It is widely distributed in south-eastern Queensland and [disjunctly](/wiki/Disjunct_distribution "Disjunct distribution") in the [White Mountains National Park](/wiki/White_Mountains_National_Park "White Mountains National Park"). In New South Wales it mainly occurs in coastal areas south from [Coffs Harbour](/wiki/Coffs_Harbour "Coffs Harbour") to [Narooma](/wiki/Narooma,_New_South_Wales "Narooma, New South Wales") and as far west as the [Blue Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Mountains,_New_South_Wales "Blue Mountains, New South Wales").[[3]](#cite_note-RBGS-3)[[4]](#cite_note-ABRS-4)[[5]](#cite_note-CANBR-5)
It is found from sea level to an altitude of 300 m (1,000 ft) in areas of predominantly summer rainfall receiving 600 to 1,200 mm (25 to 45 in) a year. Maximum temperatures across its range vary from 25 to 35 °C (77 to 95 °F) and minimum temperatures from 0 to 8 °C (32 to 46 °F), with anywhere from 0 to 50 days of frost.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6)

In Victoria it is a commonly planted ornamental and is [naturalised](/wiki/Naturalisation_(biology) "Naturalisation (biology)") in some places.[[5]](#cite_note-CANBR-5)[[22]](#cite_note-RBGV-22)

It grows in open forest and woodland, in association with such species as Sydney peppermint (*[Eucalyptus piperita](/wiki/Eucalyptus_piperita "Eucalyptus piperita")*), bangalay (*[E. botryoides](/wiki/Eucalyptus_botryoides "Eucalyptus botryoides")*),[[23]](#cite_note-Ben98-23) grey gum (*[E. punctata](/wiki/Eucalyptus_punctata "Eucalyptus punctata")*), blue-leaved stringybark (*[E. agglomerata](/wiki/Eucalyptus_agglomerata "Eucalyptus agglomerata")*), white mahogany (*[E. acmenoides](/wiki/Eucalyptus_acmenoides "Eucalyptus acmenoides")*), broad-leaved white mahogany (*[E. umbra](/wiki/Eucalyptus_umbra "Eucalyptus umbra")*), large-fruited red mahogany (*[E. scias](/wiki/Eucalyptus_scias "Eucalyptus scias")*), red bloodwood (*[Corymbia gummifera](/wiki/Corymbia_gummifera "Corymbia gummifera")*), yellow bloodwood (*[C. eximia](/wiki/Corymbia_eximia "Corymbia eximia")*), pink bloodwood (*[C. intermedia](/wiki/Corymbia_intermedia "Corymbia intermedia")*) lemon-scented gum (*[C. citriodora](/wiki/Corymbia_citriodora "Corymbia citriodora")*),[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) turpentine (*[Syncarpia glomulifera](/wiki/Syncarpia_glomulifera "Syncarpia glomulifera")*), forest oak (*[Allocasuarina torulosa](/wiki/Allocasuarina_torulosa "Allocasuarina torulosa")*),[[23]](#cite_note-Ben98-23) and cypress pines (*[Callitris](/wiki/Callitris "Callitris")* species),[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) and in hind dune communities with wedding bush (*[Monotoca elliptica](/wiki/Monotoca_elliptica "Monotoca elliptica")*) and burrawang (*[Macrozamia communis](/wiki/Macrozamia_communis "Macrozamia communis")*).[[23]](#cite_note-Ben98-23)

## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angophora_costata&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ecology")]

*Angophora costata* trees can live over 100 years. The species responds to bushfire by resprouting from epicormic buds and its lignotuber; shoots on branches of older specimens arise after a month, while shoots resprout from the trunk and base of young plants within a month of a hot fire. Seedlings appear within 9 to 10 months.[[23]](#cite_note-Ben98-23)

The tree is parasitised by the long-flowered mistletoe (*[Dendrophthoe vitellina](/wiki/Dendrophthoe_vitellina "Dendrophthoe vitellina")*).[[23]](#cite_note-Ben98-23)

The flowers are eaten by the [grey-headed flying fox](/wiki/Grey-headed_flying_fox "Grey-headed flying fox") (*Pteropus poliocephalus*) and [little red flying fox](/wiki/Little_red_flying_fox "Little red flying fox") (*P. scapulatus*), while the seed is eaten by the [crimson rosella](/wiki/Crimson_rosella "Crimson rosella") (*Platycercus elegans*).[[23]](#cite_note-Ben98-23)

Although the bark of *A. costata* is smooth, many invertebrates are found there. Several spiders in the [family Salticidae](/wiki/Jumping_spiders "Jumping spiders"), including *[Servaea vestita](/wiki/Servaea_vestita "Servaea vestita")*, and [pseudoscorpions](/wiki/Pseudoscorpion "Pseudoscorpion") live under loose bark. The flattened bug *[Stenocotis depressa](/wiki/Stenocotis_depressa "Stenocotis depressa")* evades detection by camouflage, and cockroaches and [huntsman spiders](/wiki/Huntsman_spider "Huntsman spider") live under the loose bark at the base of the trunk.[[24]](#cite_note-Child-24)

*Angophora costata* is also susceptible to the fungal pathogens *[Quambalaria pitotheka](/w/index.php?title=Quambalaria_pitotheka&action=edit&redlink=1 "Quambalaria pitotheka (page does not exist)")*, *[Aulographina eucalypti](/w/index.php?title=Aulographina_eucalypti&action=edit&redlink=1 "Aulographina eucalypti (page does not exist)")* and *[Cryptococcus neoformans](/wiki/Cryptococcus_neoformans "Cryptococcus neoformans")*.[[25]](#cite_note-Keane_2000-25)

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angophora_costata&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Uses")]

### Use in horticulture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angophora_costata&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Use in horticulture")]

Smooth-barked apple grows well in a variety of situations and can be easily grown from seed in a loose, well-drained seed-raising mixture. Some specimens have a straight trunk while others have a more branching habit with twisted trunks. The tree sometimes sheds branches and should not be planted close to buildings.[[26]](#cite_note-ANBG-26)

### Timber

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angophora_costata&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Timber")]

The heartwood is hard though not durable. Pale pink-brown in colour, it has a density of 755–1045 kg/m3 and a coarse texture. It is not suitable for use on the ground,[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) though it has been used for fencing and rough flooring.[[27]](#cite_note-FoA-27) The pale sapwood has a width of up to 5 cm (2 in) and is susceptible to attack by borers of the genus *[Lyctus](/wiki/Lyctus_(beetle) "Lyctus (beetle)")*.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6)

### Honey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angophora_costata&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Honey")]

This tree produces abundant pollen but little nectar. and the honey produced is not highly regarded.[[27]](#cite_note-FoA-27)

## Significant individuals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angophora_costata&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Significant individuals")]

[Angophora Reserve](/wiki/Angophora_Reserve "Angophora Reserve") in the [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") suburb of [Avalon](/wiki/Avalon,_New_South_Wales "Avalon, New South Wales") was named after a huge individual, reportedly around 300 years of age. That tree died in the late 20th century.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Also in Sydney, the upper [Lane Cove River](/wiki/Lane_Cove_River "Lane Cove River") Valley has several large Sydney red gums, one near Conscript Pass was measured at 45 metres tall.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The largest known living tree in New Zealand (241 cm diameter) is located at [Hobsonville](/wiki/Hobsonville "Hobsonville") near Auckland.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Gallery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Angophora_costata&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Gallery")]

* Features of *Angophora costata*
* [![Adult leaves](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Angophora_costata_-_leaves_%28adult%29.jpg/120px-Angophora_costata_-_leaves_%28adult%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Angophora_costata_-_leaves_(adult).jpg "Adult leaves")
